⚔️ Competitive Battlecards 8 COMPETITORS · Orion Investigation Platform · Updated Apr 2026
Orion competes across three buyer segments: Law Enforcement (i2, Maltego, Palantir), Enterprise Security / SOC (Splunk, Anomali, ThreatConnect, ThreatQ), and Emerging / Regional (Legion Security). Use vertical-specific positioning — the same Orion wins differently in each segment.
⚔️ IBM i2 ANALYST'S NOTEBOOK
PRIMARY LE COMPETITOR — 30-year incumbent in most global agencies
✓
Why Orion Wins

• AI vs manual — i2 requires analysts to manually draw every node and link. Orion AI maps the entire transaction network in seconds.

• Crypto-native — i2 has no native blockchain data. Requires expensive third-party feeds and manual import. Orion ingests 50+ chains natively.

• Speed — i2 investigation: days to weeks. Orion: minutes to hours. FBI CCIU cut investigation time 90%.

• Dark web integration — i2 requires separate Maltego/OSINT tools and manual import. Orion monitors Telegram, dark web markets, forums in a single pane.

• Collaboration — i2 is single-analyst desktop software. Orion is cloud-collaborative — teams share case progress in real time.

• Modern UI — i2 hasn't been redesigned in 15 years. New investigators refuse to use it. Orion adoption rate: 94% within 30 days.

⚠
Why Orion Loses

• 30-year incumbency — i2 is the FBI, NCA, Europol standard. Every analyst trained on it since their career began. Switching requires budget, retraining, political capital.

• Non-crypto cases — For traditional financial crime or non-crypto investigations, i2's manual flexibility is an advantage. Orion is crypto-first.

• IBM procurement — Large agencies have IBM enterprise agreements. Adding Orion means a new vendor.

• Chart export — i2 charts are court-admissible exhibits in many jurisdictions with established legal precedent. Orion's outputs are newer.

• Supervisor familiarity — Senior investigators who supervise junior analysts all learned i2. Change management required.

🛡️
Objection Handling

• "We've used i2 for 20 years"
→ "I know — and i2 built the playbook for link analysis. But when your investigators are tracing DPRK crypto through 14 chains and 3 mixer services, how long does building that i2 chart take? Our POC bet: Orion closes the same case 10× faster. We'll prove it in 30 days."

• "Our analysts know i2"
→ "Our average onboarding is 4 hours. The AI does what took months to learn. We'll run both in parallel — zero disruption during transition."

• "It's the agency standard"
→ "The NCA, Swedish National Police, and DEA made the same decision in the last 18 months. The standard is shifting because crypto crime shifted. i2 was built before Bitcoin existed."

🎣
Trap Questions

Ask prospect to pose to i2:

• "Trace this Bitcoin transaction through Tornado Cash, across 3 chains, and identify the final off-ramp exchange — without leaving your tool." (i2 can't — no native blockchain data)

• "Show me all Telegram channels that mentioned this wallet address in the last 30 days." (Requires separate Maltego + manual import)

• "How long would it take to build an investigation chart on a case involving 847 wallet addresses?" (i2: days. Orion: minutes)

• "Can two analysts work on the same case simultaneously?" (i2 is single-user desktop)

• "When was your blockchain data layer last updated?" (i2 has no native crypto data)

Intel: IBM i2 ANB licensing $25K–$150K per seat (perpetual + maintenance). Cloud version still limited. IBM acquired i2 in 2011 — no major product investment since. Most LE agencies on i2 8.9.x (2019 release). Named wins vs i2: FBI CCIU, NCA UK, DEA Intelligence, Swedish National Police, AUSTRAC.
⚔️ PALANTIR
ENTERPRISE / NATSEC — Gotham (gov) + Foundry (commercial). Expensive, complex, slow to deploy
✓
Why Orion Wins

• Purpose-built vs general — Palantir Gotham is a data integration and analysis platform. Requires extensive configuration for crypto. Orion is ready on day 1.

• Deployment speed — Palantir: 6–18 months to stand up. Orion: 2-week POC, production in 30 days.

• Cost — Palantir government contracts start at $5M+. Orion is 80–95% cheaper for equivalent crypto investigation capability.

• Crypto-native — Palantir has no native blockchain data. Requires TRM Labs (!) or Chainalysis as a data feed — Orion includes it all.

• LE accessibility — Palantir targets IC/DoD ($100M+ contracts). Most LE agencies can't access or afford it. Orion is deployable at county sheriff level.

⚠
Why Orion Loses

• DoD/IC dominance — Palantir has 20-year relationships at NSA, CIA, DIA, DoD. Entrenched in national security fabric.

• Data breadth — Palantir can integrate ANY data source. For agencies that want everything in one platform (signals, financial, geospatial, comms), Palantir is the platform.

• Congressional relationships — Palantir lobbied and sold through every Congressional defense committee for two decades.

• AIP (AI Platform) — Palantir's new AI narrative is compelling for CIOs. "AI on your data" is a strong message even if delivery is slow.

• Brand prestige — "We use Palantir" is a status signal for senior government officials. Hard to displace on non-rational grounds.

🛡️
Objection Handling

• "We're evaluating Palantir"
→ "Palantir and Orion solve different problems. Palantir is a data integration platform — it ingests everything and lets analysts explore. Orion is an investigation engine — it actively solves crypto crimes. Many of our customers use both: Palantir for data management, Orion for the actual investigation. What's the specific crypto crime problem you're trying to solve?"

• "Palantir has more capability"
→ "For general intelligence fusion, yes. For crypto crime investigation specifically, Orion does more on day 1 than Palantir does after 18 months of configuration. When's your next major crypto case? Let's run a POC against it."

• "Palantir AIP does AI"
→ "AIP is AI on your structured data. Orion's AI traces cross-chain transactions, identifies actor patterns, monitors dark web in real-time, and generates court-quality investigation narratives. These are different AI problems."

🎣
Trap Questions

• "How long until we can run a live crypto investigation in your platform?" (Palantir: 12–18 months + integration cost. Orion: 2 weeks)

• "What's the total contract cost including implementation, data feeds, and training for a 20-person crypto investigation team?" (Palantir: $5M–$15M. Orion: fraction)

• "Does your platform include live blockchain data for 50+ chains, dark web monitoring, and AI-generated investigation narratives out of the box?" (Palantir: no on all three — all require configuration and third-party purchases)

• "Show me how you'd trace a DPRK Lazarus Group operation across Bitcoin, Ethereum, and TRON in real time." (Palantir would need TRM Labs data feed — which IS Orion)

Intel: Palantir Gotham contracts typically $5M–$50M+, multi-year. AIP platform $2M+ minimum. Palantir actually purchases TRM Labs blockchain data as a feed — meaning Orion's data layer is inside Palantir deployments. Use this: "Your Palantir deployment likely already runs on TRM data." Win angle: "Palantir is your data warehouse. Orion is your investigation engine. They're additive."
⚔️ MALTEGO
OSINT PLATFORM — Transform-based entity mapping. Strong for open-source, weak on crypto and dark web depth
✓
Why Orion Wins

• Crypto depth — Maltego's blockchain transforms (Blockchain.info, Etherscan) are shallow. Orion has native 50+ chain tracing with actor attribution built in.

• AI analysis — Maltego shows you the graph. Orion tells you what it means. "This cluster is linked to Lazarus Group with 87% confidence."

• Dark web monitoring — Maltego can query some dark web sources via transforms. Orion actively monitors 400+ dark web markets and Telegram channels in real time.

• Workflow — Maltego is exploration. Orion is end-to-end investigation with case management, timelines, and reporting.

• Speed at scale — Maltego slows significantly at large graphs (1000+ nodes). Orion handles investigations with millions of transactions.

⚠
Why Orion Loses

• OSINT breadth — Maltego has 2000+ data transforms across social media, DNS, WHOIS, email, companies. For surface web OSINT, nothing beats it.

• Price point — Maltego One $999/month per analyst. Accessible for small teams with tight budgets who need OSINT basics.

• Community — Large community of custom transforms. Analysts share and build on each other's work.

• Non-crypto use cases — For fraud investigations, corporate intel, or social media investigations without crypto, Maltego is the right tool.

• Integration flexibility — Maltego connects to almost any data source via custom transforms.

🛡️
Objection Handling

• "Our analysts already use Maltego for OSINT"
→ "Maltego and Orion are complementary — Maltego for surface web OSINT, Orion for on-chain and dark web investigation. Many of our customers use both. The question is: when the investigation goes on-chain, what do you use? If it's Maltego, how long does building a Bitcoin transaction graph take vs an Orion AI trace?"

• "Maltego is cheaper"
→ "For surface OSINT, yes. But Maltego with a blockchain data transform subscription + dark web data + manual analysis hours — the total cost of investigation is actually higher. Orion bundles it all and the AI eliminates 90% of manual work."

• "We're comfortable with Maltego"
→ "Comfort with the tool is real. But how comfortable are your investigators with the results? Show me a DPRK ransomware case that Maltego closed."

🎣
Trap Questions

• "Trace $2M from this Bitcoin address through a Tornado Cash mixer to a Binance off-ramp — in real time." (Maltego's blockchain transforms lack this depth)

• "Monitor all Telegram channels mentioning this wallet address and alert me within 60 seconds of a new mention." (Maltego can't do real-time monitoring)

• "What does this entity's transaction pattern tell you about whether it's a sanctioned actor?" (Maltego shows nodes; Orion gives you an answer)

• "Can your platform generate a court-quality investigation report from a raw transaction hash?" (Maltego: no built-in reporting. Orion: one click)

• "How many blockchain transactions can your graph handle before performance degrades?" (Maltego: ~5,000 nodes. Orion: millions)

Intel: Maltego pricing: One $999/mo, Pro $1,999/mo, Enterprise custom. Data transforms cost extra ($200–$1,000/mo per source). Positioning: "Maltego is your OSINT layer. Orion is your crypto investigation layer. Together they cover the full digital crime spectrum." Position as complementary, not competing — then win the budget argument on ROI.
⚔️ ANOMALI
THREAT INTEL AGGREGATION — ThreatStream platform. Different buyer (SOC/CISO) vs Orion (investigators/LE)
✓
Why Orion Wins

• Investigation vs intel feeds — Anomali aggregates threat intelligence indicators (IOCs, TTPs). Orion actively investigates financial crime using that intelligence. They're sequential tools.

• Crypto depth — Anomali's crypto coverage is limited to hash/wallet IOCs in threat feeds. Orion traces entire transaction networks with actor attribution.

• LE relevance — Anomali is built for enterprise SOC teams. Orion is built for investigators. Law enforcement agencies don't buy Anomali.

• Actionability — Anomali says "this IP is associated with Lazarus Group." Orion says "follow this transaction chain to identify the exchange used and the withdrawing account."

• Dark web — Anomali monitors dark web for IOCs. Orion monitors for wallet addresses, crypto transactions, actor communications.

⚠
Why Orion Loses

• SIEM integration — Anomali integrates with Splunk, QRadar, Microsoft Sentinel. Enterprise security teams want their TI platform connected to their SIEM. Orion doesn't compete here.

• ISAC feeds — Anomali has established relationships with all major ISACs (FS-ISAC, H-ISAC, etc.). For organisations in regulated verticals needing ISAC data, Anomali is the standard.

• Indicator management — For large enterprises managing millions of IOCs, Anomali ThreatStream's indicator lifecycle management is best-in-class.

• Existing security stack — If an org already uses Anomali for TI, adding Orion requires justifying a separate crypto investigation budget.

🛡️
Objection Handling

• "We use Anomali for threat intelligence"
→ "Perfect — and Anomali is excellent at it. Orion is what your financial crime investigation team uses when Anomali's threat feed surfaces a crypto-linked actor. Anomali detects. Orion investigates. Do you have investigators as well as a SOC?"

• "We don't need another tool"
→ "What happens when your AML team flags a suspicious crypto transaction? Does Anomali tell your investigators where the money went? That's the gap Orion fills — and it's the gap that's resulting in SAR dead-ends for your team right now."

• "Same budget, different tools"
→ "Anomali is OpEx for your SOC. Orion is budget for your financial crime/fraud investigation team. They report to different leaders. Let's get your Head of Investigations on the call."

🎣
Trap Questions

• "When your threat feed surfaces a Bitcoin wallet linked to a ransomware group, what does your investigator do next?" (Anomali identifies the IOC; it can't trace the funds — that's Orion)

• "Can your platform follow crypto proceeds from a ransomware payment through mixing services to final exchange withdrawal?" (No — Anomali doesn't do transaction tracing)

• "How many of your financial crime investigations last year resulted in a SAR or arrest?" (Anomali can't answer this — it's not an investigation platform)

• "Show me the DPRK actor profile behind this wallet address, including their associated infrastructure, past victims, and laundering pattern." (Anomali: shows IOC tags. Orion: comprehensive actor dossier)

Intel: Anomali ThreatStream $100K–$500K/yr enterprise. Key differentiator: always position as additive. The CISO buys Anomali. The Head of Investigations buys Orion. These are separate conversations with separate budgets. Win: get a meeting with the investigation team lead separately from the SOC conversation.
⚔️ THREATCONNECT
TI OPERATIONS + SOAR — Threat intel management with playbook automation. Enterprise and government
✓
Why Orion Wins

• Crypto investigation depth — ThreatConnect manages threat intel workflows. Orion actively investigates blockchain crime. Fundamentally different capabilities.

• LE use case — ThreatConnect is an enterprise/government TI sharing platform. Not designed for LE investigators tracing crypto flows.

• AI investigation — ThreatConnect's AI automates indicator enrichment and playbook triggers. Orion's AI investigates financial crime and generates prosecution narratives.

• Speed to value — ThreatConnect requires significant playbook configuration. Orion works out of the box for crypto investigations on day 1.

⚠
Why Orion Loses

• TI sharing communities — ThreatConnect has ISAC integrations and trusted sharing groups. For organisations where peer TI sharing is a priority, it's compelling.

• SOAR automation — For SOC teams wanting automated response playbooks (block IP, quarantine host), ThreatConnect's SOAR capability is real.

• Existing TI investment — If an org has built workflows in ThreatConnect, switching or adding tools requires justification at the CISO/SecOps level.

• Government clearance — ThreatConnect has FedRAMP authorization and handles classified TI at some agencies.

🛡️
Objection Handling

• "We use ThreatConnect for our TI operations"
→ "ThreatConnect manages your threat intelligence library. Orion investigates the financial crime behind the threats. When a ransomware IOC surfaces in ThreatConnect — who traces the crypto payment to recover funds and build the prosecution case? That's the Orion use case."

• "We want one platform"
→ "ThreatConnect is your TI operations hub. Orion is your crypto investigation engine. They serve different teams — your SOC vs your financial crime investigators. Is your investigation team currently using ThreatConnect for crypto tracing?"

• "ThreatConnect has playbook automation"
→ "SOAR playbooks automate your defensive response. Orion automates the investigation — from transaction hash to prosecution-ready dossier. These are two different automation problems."

🎣
Trap Questions

• "Show me how your platform traces $500K in ransomware payments through 5 wallets to the final exchange withdrawal." (ThreatConnect can't — it manages indicators, not transaction graphs)

• "How does your platform identify a DPRK-linked wallet cluster from behavioural patterns across 10,000 transactions?" (ThreatConnect: tag management and indicator enrichment. Orion: AI pattern matching)

• "Can your platform generate a court-admissible financial investigation report from a suspicious transaction?" (No — ThreatConnect is not an investigation platform)

• "How many financial crime prosecutions has your platform contributed to?" (ThreatConnect: defensive security focus. Orion: hundreds of active prosecutions globally)

Intel: ThreatConnect TI Ops $75K–$300K/yr. SOAR module adds cost. Strategy: Always route to investigation team, not SOC. SOC owns ThreatConnect. Investigation team owns Orion. Different budgets, different conversations, different champions.
⚔️ THREATQ
THREAT INTEL PLATFORM — Collaborative investigation and TI management. Mid-market focus, MSSPs
✓
Why Orion Wins

• Crypto investigation — ThreatQ has no blockchain analysis capability. For any crypto-involved investigation, Orion is the only option.

• AI depth — ThreatQ uses ML for indicator scoring and prioritisation. Orion uses LLMs for active investigation, pattern recognition, and narrative generation.

• Investigation workflow — ThreatQ is collaborative TI management. Orion is end-to-end investigation platform with case management from incident to prosecution.

• Scale — ThreatQ optimised for MSSPs and mid-market. Orion scales to global LE agencies handling millions of cases.

⚠
Why Orion Loses

• MSSP channel — ThreatQ is heavily distributed through MSSPs. If a prospect uses an MSSP that bundles ThreatQ, it's sticky.

• Collaborative workflows — ThreatQ's team collaboration features for sharing indicators and investigations across analysts are mature.

• On-premise option — ThreatQ offers on-prem for air-gapped environments. Some government agencies require this.

• Price — ThreatQ is positioned below enterprise TI platforms on price. Budget-constrained teams may choose it.

🛡️
Objection Handling

• "We use ThreatQ for investigations"
→ "ThreatQ manages your investigation workflow and indicator sharing. When the investigation involves cryptocurrency — which in 2026 is most financial crime — what does your team use to trace the funds? That's where Orion sits."

• "Our MSSP includes ThreatQ"
→ "Your MSSP handles your managed detection. Orion handles your crypto crime investigation — that's typically owned by your in-house investigation team, not outsourced. Who leads your financial crime investigations?"

• "We want one platform"
→ "For traditional TI operations, ThreatQ is a solid choice. For crypto crime investigation, no TI platform competes with a purpose-built investigation engine. The question isn't which platform — it's which problem are you solving today?"

🎣
Trap Questions

• "Show me how your platform traces a $1M crypto payment through a cross-chain bridge to a sanctioned exchange." (ThreatQ: no blockchain capability)

• "Can your platform monitor 400+ dark web markets for mentions of specific wallet addresses in real time?" (ThreatQ: indicator-based dark web monitoring only)

• "How does your AI distinguish between a retail Bitcoin user and a DPRK-controlled wallet cluster?" (ThreatQ: rule-based scoring. Orion: LLM behavioural analysis)

• "What's your average time from suspicious transaction to prosecution-ready report?" (ThreatQ: not an investigation-to-prosecution platform)

Intel: ThreatQ pricing $50K–$200K/yr. Primarily mid-market, MSSPs, and government contractors. Positioning: ThreatQ owns the TI management workflow; Orion owns the crypto investigation workflow. Complementary in the same stack.
⚔️ SPLUNK
SIEM / SOAR — Ubiquitous security platform. Not a direct competitor — same budget, different problem
✓
Why Orion Wins

• Purpose-built crypto — Splunk ingests logs. Orion investigates crypto crime. You can build Splunk dashboards for blockchain data with massive custom development; Orion works on day 1.

• Investigation vs monitoring — Splunk tells you something happened. Orion tells you who did it, where the money went, and what to do next.

• LE use case — Splunk has zero meaningful law enforcement deployments for crypto investigation. Orion owns the space.

• ROI on crypto cases — A Splunk blockchain dashboard requires 6+ months of engineering. Orion closes cases in the first week.

⚠
Why Orion Loses

• Universal deployment — Splunk is in 90% of large enterprises and government agencies. "We already have Splunk" is the most common objection in enterprise sales.

• DIY mindset — Large security teams believe they can build anything in Splunk. CISOs with strong engineering teams resist buying specialised tools.

• Budget holder — Splunk is owned by the CISO/SecOps budget. If Orion gets categorised as "security tooling," it competes with the Splunk renewal.

• Cisco acquisition — Cisco acquired Splunk (2024). Expanded government channel and deeper federal relationships.

🛡️
Objection Handling

• "We already have Splunk — we'll just build it there"
→ "How long will that take? Our fastest-moving competitor started building crypto dashboards in Splunk 18 months ago. They're still in development. Meanwhile your investigators are losing cases because the money is moving faster than your Splunk build. What's the cost of one unresolved $2M ransomware case?"

• "Everything goes through our SIEM"
→ "Orion doesn't replace your SIEM — it integrates with it. Investigation findings flow back into Splunk. Your security team keeps their workflow. Your investigators get a purpose-built tool."

• "We don't want another vendor"
→ "Understood. What if I showed you the ROI on one closed ransomware case — typically $500K–$2M recovered — compared to Orion's annual cost? The first investigation pays for the tool."

🎣
Trap Questions

• "How long would it take your team to build a cross-chain transaction tracing capability in Splunk that covers 50 blockchains?" (18–36 months minimum, if ever)

• "Does your Splunk deployment include live DPRK actor attribution for blockchain addresses?" (No — Splunk has no blockchain threat intelligence)

• "When you ingest a blockchain transaction log, can Splunk automatically identify it as belonging to a Lazarus Group wallet cluster?" (Requires custom ML development. Orion does it natively)

• "What's the cost of building and maintaining a crypto investigation capability in Splunk vs purchasing Orion?" (Engineering cost alone exceeds Orion's annual cost in year 1)

Key strategy: Never let Orion get categorised as a "security tool" competing with Splunk. Orion is a "financial crime investigation platform" budgeted under the Head of Investigations, not the CISO. Change the budget conversation early. Integration story: "Orion + Splunk = complete coverage. Splunk monitors your security posture. Orion investigates the financial crime."
⚔️ LEGION SECURITY
REGIONAL COMPETITOR — Emerging cyber investigation platform. EMEA-focused, government and LE
✓
Why Orion Wins

• Scale and data depth — TRM Labs processes billions of blockchain transactions. Legion Security's dataset is a fraction of TRM's coverage and recency.

• AI maturity — Orion's LLM-powered investigation capability is multiple years ahead of emerging regional vendors.

• Global actor intelligence — TRM Labs has the deepest DPRK, Russian, Iranian crypto actor attribution database globally. Regional vendors lack this breadth.

• Proven track record — FBI, NCA, Europol, AUSTRAC. References from the world's top agencies are impossible for emerging competitors to match.

• Regulatory alignment — TRM's compliance team tracks all major regulatory frameworks globally. Regional competitors may cover local regs only.

⚠
Why Orion Loses

• Local relationships — Regional vendors have direct government relationships, local language support, and in-country presence that TRM is still building.

• Data sovereignty — Some EMEA governments require data to stay in-country or in-region. Regional vendors may offer local hosting TRM cannot.

• Local compliance — Regional vendors may be pre-approved for local procurement frameworks (G-Cloud UK, French UGAP, etc.).

• Price — Emerging regional vendors often undercut on price to gain market share. Short-term discounting can win budget-constrained deals.

🛡️
Objection Handling

• "Legion is local and understands our market"
→ "Local presence matters for support. Orion's threat actor intelligence is global — and your threat actors certainly aren't local. The DPRK group targeting your banks operates from Pyongyang and launders through Singapore. Does Legion have intelligence on that specific group?"

• "They're cheaper"
→ "Today, yes. But when you're standing in front of a judge needing to prove provenance of seized crypto from a Russian ransomware group, which investigation platform's evidence has precedent? Ask them for their court case references."

• "We want to support local vendors"
→ "Completely understood — we respect that policy. Where data sovereignty or local presence is an absolute requirement, let's talk about our EMEA deployment options and regional partners."

🎣
Trap Questions

• "How many global law enforcement agencies use your platform to actively investigate DPRK, Russian, and Iranian crypto operations?" (Legion: limited global LE footprint. Orion: FBI, NCA, Europol, AUSTRAC, DEA)

• "Can you show me attribution data for the Lazarus Group subgroup responsible for the Bybit hack?" (Regional vendors lack this intelligence depth)

• "What's your coverage across all major blockchains including emerging L2s and privacy coins?" (Regional vendors typically lag TRM's 50+ chain coverage)

• "Show me your court case references — specifically cases where your investigation output was admitted as evidence." (Orion has multiple international references; emerging vendors cannot match this)

Strategy: Never dismiss regional competitors — they know local procurement, local language, local relationships. Win on: global intelligence depth, proven prosecutions, and AI maturity. Lose gracefully on: data sovereignty requirements (escalate to TRM EMEA team for local deployment options).

🌍Country Intelligence — Cyber Threats · Crypto Crime · Regulatory Posture28 COUNTRIES · ORION-ADDRESSABLE
Per-country intelligence for cyber + crypto sales motions: ransomware actor presence, dark-web forum activity, threat-actor TTPs, OSINT investigation needs, regulatory mandates (NIS2, CIRCIA, BSA, MiCA, BitLicense), key agencies (CERTs, LE, intelligence, regulators, tax authorities), and where Orion\'s 7 investigation skills create asymmetric value vs incumbents (i2, Palantir, Maltego, Chainalysis Reactor).
28 shown
💡 Click any country card to open its country×vertical playbook in the Easy Button (33 countries with detailed playbooks supported)
🇬🇧
United Kingdom

Regs: Computer Misuse Act, NIS2, DCMS Cyber Bill, Economic Crime Act

Threats: NHS ransomware, DPRK/Russia intrusions, IAB ecosystem, darknet fraud

Agencies: NCA, Met Police, GCHQ/NCSC, HMRC/FCA, Home Office

Landscape: i2 (GCHQ), Palantir (Met eval), TRM (banks)

🇫🇷
France

Regs: LPM 2024-2030, NIS2, RGPD/CNIL, AML6D

Threats: State-sponsored OIV attacks, hospital ransomware, darknet drug trafficking

Agencies: ANSSI, DGSI, Gendarmerie C3N, OCLCTIC, Tracfin

Landscape: i2 (French LE standard), TRM (banks)

🇦🇪
UAE

Regs: Cybercrime Law 34/2021, NESA CII, CBUAE Virtual Asset Regulation, ADGM/DIFC VASP

Threats: Iran/Russia nation-state, financial crime hub, ransomware, hawala

Agencies: UAE SIGN, Dubai Police, Abu Dhabi Police, CBUAE, NCC

Landscape: Palantir (gov), TRM (police)

🇦🇺
Australia

Regs: SOCI Act 2018, AA Act 2018, AML/CTF Tranche 2, ASD Essential Eight

Threats: Chinese espionage, healthcare ransomware, crypto compliance, BEC fraud

Agencies: AFP, AUSTRAC, ASD/ACSC, ASIO, State Police

Landscape: i2 (AFP standard), TRM (APAC), Palantir (eval)

🇩🇪
Germany

Regs: NIS2 Umsetzungsgesetz (30K+ entities), BSI Amendment, BaFin MiCAR, GwG AML

Threats: Russian APT (Sandworm, APT28), Mittelstand ransomware, industrial espionage

Agencies: BKA SO4, BSI, BND, BaFin, ZIT Frankfurt

Competitors: i2 (BKA standard), Elliptic (banks), Palantir (eval)

🇺🇸
United States

Regs: CIRCIA 2022, CLOUD Act, BSA/AML, FinCEN Crypto Guidance, SEC Cyber Rules

Threats: DPRK Lazarus, Russian RaaS (LockBit/ALPHV), Chinese APT, fentanyl crypto networks, romance scam syndicates

Agencies: FBI CCIU, HSI, DEA, IRS-CI, USSS, ATF, FinCEN, OFAC, CISA, NSA

Landscape: Chainalysis (incumbent), CipherTrace/Mastercard, TRM Screening (internal)

🇨🇦
Canada

Regs: PCMLTFA AML/CTF, PIPEDA/Bill C-27, CRTC Cyber, FINTRAC Virtual Currency Rules

Threats: Russian financial crime, Chinese espionage, domestic ransomware, drug network crypto

Agencies: RCMP Cybercrime, CSIS, FINTRAC, CSE, CBSA, OPP, Sûreté du Québec

Landscape: Chainalysis (RCMP), i2 (standard), Elliptic (FINTRAC)

🇳🇱
Netherlands

Regs: NIS2, DORA, MiCAR, WWFT AML, AFM Crypto Register

Threats: Darknet market infrastructure (Hydra successor), ransomware hosting, TRON-based laundering

Agencies: Europol EC3 (HQ), FIOD, KLPD, AIVD, DNB

Landscape: Chainalysis (Europol), i2 (FIOD), Maltego (OSINT)

🇸🇬
Singapore

Regs: PSA 2019 (MAS), MAS Notice MAS-N02, VASP licensing mandatory, AML/CFT Notice 626

Threats: Money laundering through crypto (Oct 2023 $2.8B bust), scam call centres, DPRK sanctions evasion

Agencies: SPF Commercial Affairs Dept, MAS, CPIB, ISD, CAD

Landscape: Elliptic (MAS), Chainalysis (SPF), TRM strong in exchanges

🇯🇵
Japan

Regs: FISC Act (FSA), PSA 2017 (VASP), AML/CFT Act, Revised Funds Settlement Act 2023

Threats: Lazarus exchange hacks (Bybit, Coincheck $530M), yakuza crypto, North Korean IT workers

Agencies: NPA Cybercrime Division, FSA, JAFIC, METI, NISC

Landscape: Chainalysis (NPA), local vendors (NTT), limited Orion penetration — high opportunity

🇰🇷
South Korea

Regs: VASP Act 2021 (KOFIU), MSIT Cyber Framework, Act on Information and Communications Network

Threats: DPRK (highest per-capita crypto exposure), BTC-e style laundering, P2P scam networks

Agencies: KFIU, NIS Cybersecurity, National Police Cybercrime, FSS, FIU

Landscape: Korean domestic vendors, Chainalysis (limited), strong whitespace for Orion

🇮🇱
Israel

Regs: Cyber Defence Regulation, Bank of Israel Crypto Directive, CASC Framework, AML Prohibition Law

Threats: Hamas/Hezbollah crypto financing, Iranian APT, tech startup espionage, ransomware groups

Agencies: ISA (Shin Bet), IDF Unit 8200/SIGINT, IADB (BITS), Israel Money Laundering Authority

Landscape: Strong domestic cyber ecosystem, TRM expanding via Tal Croitoru, NSO Group adjacency

🇨🇭
Switzerland

Regs: FINMA Crypto Framework, AMLA (Anti-Money Laundering), DLT Act 2021, nLPD data privacy

Threats: Crypto valley regulatory arbitrage, Russian oligarch asset flows, darknet financial layers

Agencies: fedpol, MROS (money reporting), FINMA, NDB (intelligence), Zurich Cantonal Police

Landscape: Elliptic (Swiss banks), Chainalysis (FINMA supervised entities), Crypto Valley opportunity

🇸🇪
Sweden

Regs: NIS2 (early implementer), DORA, MiCAR, AMLA 5AMLD, Finansinspektionen VASP rules

Threats: Russian-linked cybercrime, organised crime crypto, Comet ransomware group

Agencies: Swedish Police NCCU, SÄPO (security), FI (regulator), Riksbank

Landscape: TRM has Swedish National Police. Active expansion. Chainalysis losing ground

🇪🇸
Spain

Regs: NIS2, CNMV Crypto Registration, SEPBLAC AML, LOPDGDD, Royal Decree 7/2021

Threats: Colombian cartel crypto (CJNG, Sinaloa), Darknet Spanish-language markets, Russian money flows

Agencies: GDT (Guardia Civil Cybercrime), CNI, SEPBLAC, CNMV, Policía Nacional Cibercrimen

Landscape: i2 (LE standard), Elliptic (banks), Orion opportunity with Guardia Civil

🇮🇹
Italy

Regs: NIS2, DORA, OAM Crypto Register, Banca d'Italia AML, PNRR cybersecurity investments

Threats: Ndrangheta/Camorra crypto laundering, ransomware on SMEs, Iranian-linked fraud

Agencies: Europol (Italy leads major ops), Guardia di Finanza, Polizia Postale, CNAIPIC, UIF

Landscape: i2 (GdF standard), limited Chainalysis, strong whitespace for Orion

🇧🇷
Brazil

Regs: Lei 14.478/2022 (Crypto Law), BCB Resolution 96, CVM Crypto Guidance, LGPD

Threats: PIX fraud networks, Brazilian cartel crypto, romance scams, drainer-as-a-service

Agencies: PF (Federal Police Cybercrime), COAF (FIU), CVM, BCB, MP Federal

Landscape: Chainalysis (limited), local vendors (Febraban), Enrique Edelstein TRM territory

🇭🇰
Hong Kong

Regs: VASP licensing (SFC mandatory 2023), AMLO Amendment, PDPO, HKMA Crypto Circular

Threats: Chinese state actors, JPEX collapse ($1.6B fraud), cross-border laundering, Tether-based crime

Agencies: HKPF CSTCB, JFIU (joint FIU), SFC, HKMA, OSCG

Landscape: Chainalysis (SFC mandated), TRM growing (HKPF), Elliptic (HKMA banks)

🇮🇳
India

Regs: PMLA (crypto covered since 2023), FIU-IND VASP registration, SEBI Crypto Framework (draft)

Threats: Pig butchering syndicates (Myanmar/Cambodia), UPI fraud networks, ransomware on PSUs

Agencies: ED (Enforcement Directorate), CBI Cybercrime, FIU-IND, I4C (MHA), CERT-In

Landscape: Limited Chainalysis, local compliance tools, high-growth opportunity as regulation matures

🇸🇦
Saudi Arabia

Regs: SAMA Crypto Framework, SDAIA Data Law, NCA Cybersecurity Controls, Vision 2030 fintech

Threats: Iranian cyber ops, regional hawala networks, oil infrastructure targeting, terror financing

Agencies: SAFIU (FIU), NCA (National Cybersecurity), SAMA, CITC, General Directorate of Investigation

Landscape: Palantir (gov contracts), Chainalysis (limited), Vision 2030 budget creates opportunity

🇮🇪
Ireland

Regs: DORA (hub for EU financial firms), CBI VASP regime, GDPR enforcement leader, AMLD5/6

Threats: EU financial crime hub (most major banks EU HQ here), ransomware on HSE, Russian money flows

Agencies: Garda National Cyber Crime Bureau, CBI, Revenue Commissioners, IAA

Landscape: Elliptic (CBI supervised banks), strong DORA compliance opportunity

🇧🇪
Belgium

Regs: NIS2, DORA, FSMA Crypto Regulation, CTIF/CFI AML, NBB Crypto Requirements

Threats: NATO-targeting espionage (Brussels = NATO HQ), port-based drug trafficking crypto, EU institution attacks

Agencies: FCCU (Federal Computer Crime Unit), CTIF, FSMA, OCAM, CCN

Landscape: Chainalysis (federal), Europol adjacency via The Hague, NATO HQ opportunity

🇩🇰
Denmark

Regs: NIS2 (early implementer), DORA, Finanstilsynet VASP rules, MiCAR, GDPR enforcer

Threats: Russian GRU (targeting energy/infrastructure), crypto ML through Nordic banks, pig butchering

Agencies: NCSC Denmark, SØIK (serious crime), Finanstilsynet, PET (intelligence), Politiet

Landscape: Strong NIS2/DORA compliance spend, Chainalysis weak in Nordics, Orion opportunity

🇳🇿
New Zealand

Regs: FSMA (VASP mandatory), AML/CFT Act 2009 Amendment, Privacy Act 2020, NZISM

Threats: Chinese espionage (Five Eyes member), domestic ransomware, DPRK crypto exposure

Agencies: NZPF Digital Crime Group, GCSB, FENZ, FIU-NZ, RBNZ

Landscape: TRM via APAC team (Eileen Ng territory), AFP alignment, Chainalysis limited presence

🇵🇱
Poland

Regs: NIS2 implementation, UKNF Crypto Register, AML Act Amendment 2021, RODO (GDPR)

Threats: Russian cyber ops (border proximity), Eastern European RaaS groups, fuel card fraud crypto

Agencies: ABW (counterintelligence), CBZC Cybercrime, UKNF, GIIF (FIU), KNF

Landscape: EU funding driving cybersecurity spend, i2 (legacy), Orion opportunity with CBZC

🇿🇦
South Africa

Regs: FICA Amendment (VASP), FSCA Crypto Licensing 2023, POPIA, Grey-listed by FATF 2023

Threats: Mirror Trader networks, Ponzi crypto schemes (MTI $589M), ransomware on state entities

Agencies: SARS (tax), FIC, SAPS Hawks, State Security Agency, SARB

Landscape: Post-greylisting compliance urgency. Chainalysis entering. Orion first-mover opportunity

🇹🇷
Turkey

Regs: Crypto Law 7518 (2024), BDDK Licensing, MASAK AML, SPK Capital Markets Act

Threats: Lira devaluation driving crypto adoption, currency sanctions evasion, Russian oligarch flows via Istanbul

Agencies: MASAK (FIU), KOM Cybercrime, BDDK, SPK, MIT (intelligence)

Landscape: 2024 Crypto Law creates licensing urgency. High exchange density. Compliance spend surging

🇲🇽
Mexico

Regs: Fintech Law 2018 (ITFS), CNBV Crypto Circular, LFPIORPI AML, SHCP guidelines

Threats: Cartel crypto networks (CJNG, Sinaloa), fentanyl financing, kidnap ransom crypto, money mules

Agencies: FGR (Cybercrime), UIF (FIU), SAT, CISEN/CNI, SSP

Landscape: DEA partnership drives US tool adoption. Enrique Edelstein territory. High cartel crypto demand

🗺️ Orion Partner Ecosystem Map Data Intelligence Network
Orion's data ecosystem is cyber-first: Flashpoint, SpyCloud, Hudson Rock, Cybersixgill, SOCRadar, Babel Street, Vetric, Aurelius, Whisper, ComplyAdvantage, Sanctions.io, Chainabuse, WHOIS XML API, Cloudburst, Ransomware.live, Overwatch, Tesseract, PB Layers — plus blockchain intel from TRM (50+ chains, 285+ bridges). Multi-source fusion vs single-source dependencies.

Orion is the AI investigation orchestration layer that connects and activates data from across the cyber intelligence ecosystem. Each tier represents a distinct intelligence dimension.

CISOVendor TrustSupply Chain

Podcast · Feb 2026 · 55 min

⚡ Orion Use Cases & Investigation Workflows ORION AI — INVESTIGATION PLATFORM

AI-powered cyber investigation and threat hunting platform — detecting IOCs, correlating adversary TTPs, mapping threat actor infrastructure, and producing court-ready intelligence packages.

🔍
IOC Detection & Threat Hunting
IOC Correlation · MITRE ATT&CK Mapping

Ingests IOCs from across the partner ecosystem and correlates them in real time against Orion's investigation graph. Analysts hunt threats proactively, mapping detections directly to MITRE ATT&CK techniques.

Investigation Flow
GreyNoise/Censys scan → Flashpoint actor profiles → Orion IOC correlation → ATT&CK tagging → Threat hunt report
GreyNoiseCensysFlashpointTeam CymruHudson Rock
Output: IOC cluster report · TTP profile · Hunt brief
🔒
Ransomware Investigation
TTP Analysis · Infrastructure Mapping · Actor Attribution

Correlates attack infrastructure — C2 servers, dropper domains, lateral movement paths — against known ransomware group profiles. Identifies the group, maps campaign infrastructure, and generates LE referral packages.

Investigation Flow
Hudson Rock creds → Flashpoint group ID → Censys/Cymru infra map → Orion actor graph → LE referral package
Hudson RockFlashpointCensysTeam Cymru
Output: Group attribution · C2 map · LE package
🌐
Nation-State APT Tracking
Campaign Attribution · Geopolitical Intelligence

Tracks nation-state actors — Lazarus, Sandworm, APT28, Volt Typhoon — by correlating evolving infrastructure, TTPs, and targeting patterns. Anticipates the next move rather than documenting the last one.

Investigation Flow
Cyjax/Flashpoint geopolitical → Team Cymru BGP routing → Censys infra scan → Orion actor graph → Pipl identity resolution
CyjaxFlashpointTeam CymruCensysPipl
Output: APT profile · Campaign map · Designation package
🔑
Credential Theft & IAB Investigation
IAB Network Mapping · Infostealer Analysis

Maps the full IAB ecosystem: which infostealers collected credentials, which forums they were listed on, who bought them, and what infrastructure was deployed post-access.

Investigation Flow
SpyCloud stolen creds → Hudson Rock infostealer ID → Flashpoint dark web listings → Censys post-access infra → Orion IAB network graph
SpyCloud Hudson RockFlashpointCensysPipl
Output: IAB ecosystem map · Buyer attribution · Enforcement targets
⛓️
Supply Chain Attack Investigation
Dependency Compromise · Backdoor Detection

Correlates IOCs from supply chain compromises (SolarWinds, XZ Utils, 3CX) across the victim ecosystem. Maps implanted backdoor infrastructure and attributes campaigns to specific threat groups.

Investigation Flow
Flashpoint early warning → GreyNoise C2 callbacks → Team Cymru exfil mapping → Orion IOC clustering → Actor attribution
FlashpointGreyNoiseTeam CymruCensysCyjax
Output: Scope map · Backdoor graph · Victim notification list
🌑
Darknet Monitoring & Early Warning
Dark Web Surveillance · Leaked Data Detection

Continuously monitors dark web forums, paste sites, and ransomware leak sites for mentions of target organisations, leaked credentials, and emerging campaigns. Early warning intelligence before an attack launches.

Investigation Flow
Flashpoint dark web → SpyCloud breach data → Hudson Rock infostealers → Orion signal correlation → Early warning brief
Flashpoint SpyCloud Hudson RockSOC RadarExorde Labs
Output: Early warning brief · Exposure prioritisation
📡
Influence Operation Investigation
Bot Network Detection · Operator Attribution

Identifies and maps coordinated influence operations — bot networks, state-sponsored disinformation, and synthetic persona networks. Produces attribution evidence for platform takedowns and government briefings.

Investigation Flow
Cyabra bot detection → Exorde narrative mapping → Cyjax geopolitical context → Censys hosting infra → Pipl identity resolution
CyabraExorde LabsCyjaxCensysPipl
Output: Bot map · Takedown package · Government briefing
🚨
Incident Response & Breach Investigation
Breach Scope · Lateral Movement · Evidence Preservation

Accelerates IR by immediately correlating attack infrastructure against known actor profiles, surfacing compromised accounts, and mapping lateral movement paths. From "something happened" to full attribution in hours.

Investigation Flow
SpyCloud/Hudson Rock creds → GreyNoise/Censys infra map → Orion actor correlation → Lateral movement timeline → Court-ready evidence
SpyCloud Hudson RockGreyNoiseCensysTeam Cymru
Output: Breach scope · Attribution · Court-ready evidence
🎤Custom Pitches by Vertical
Cyber-first pitch frameworks for every vertical. Orion is an AI-native cyber investigation platform — crypto/blockchain tracing is one capability, not the whole story.
🏛️
Government / Law Enforcement
LEAD WITH:

AI-native investigation that replaces 3-4 fragmented tools. Threat actor attribution in seconds, not weeks. Dark web intelligence from 15+ marketplaces and 200+ Telegram channels. Cross-border investigation coordination.

ADD WHEN RELEVANT:

Cryptocurrency tracing for financial crime, DPRK attribution for national security, sanctions evasion monitoring.

OPENING STAT:

"Your analysts shouldn't need 5 tools to investigate one threat actor."

🏦
Financial Services
LEAD WITH:

Proactive dark web monitoring for threats targeting your institution. AI-powered threat actor attribution for incident response. Regulatory compliance acceleration (SEC disclosure, DORA, NIS2).

ADD WHEN RELEVANT:

Crypto fraud investigation, VASP compliance, blockchain tracing for institutions with digital asset exposure.

OPENING STAT:

"How quickly can your team attribute a cyber incident to a specific threat group? Orion does it in seconds."

🏥
Healthcare
LEAD WITH:

Ransomware group attribution and dark web extortion monitoring. PHI exposure detection across dark web marketplaces. HIPAA breach response acceleration — reduce investigation time from weeks to hours.

ADD WHEN RELEVANT:

Ransom payment tracing (crypto), supply chain vendor compromise monitoring.

OPENING STAT:

"Healthcare is hit by ransomware every 2 seconds. When it's your turn, how fast can you attribute the attack?"

🏢
Enterprise / Tech / CISO
LEAD WITH:

Proactive threat intelligence — know when your org is being discussed on the dark web. Credential exposure monitoring, executive impersonation detection, supply chain compromise intelligence.

ADD WHEN RELEVANT:

Crypto-related threats for companies with Web3/DeFi exposure, competitor intelligence gathering.

OPENING STAT:

"How do you know if your company is being discussed on the dark web right now? Orion tells you in real-time."

⚡
Critical Infrastructure / Energy / OT
LEAD WITH:

Nation-state APT attribution and infrastructure mapping. OT/ICS threat monitoring on dark web forums. Regulatory compliance (NIS2, SOCI Act, CIRCIA) with investigation capability.

ADD WHEN RELEVANT:

Ransomware financial tracing for ransom payment analysis, sanctions compliance for state-affiliated threat actors.

OPENING STAT:

"Nation-state actors are actively targeting your sector. Orion attributes the attack and maps their infrastructure before they strike."

🛡️
Insurance / Cyber Underwriting
LEAD WITH:

Pre-bind risk assessment using dark web intelligence. Claims investigation acceleration with AI-powered threat actor attribution. Portfolio monitoring for emerging threat exposure.

ADD WHEN RELEVANT:

Crypto fraud claims investigation, ransomware payment tracing for recovery analysis.

OPENING STAT:

"What if you could see a policyholder's dark web exposure before binding the policy?"

📅 Regulatory Calendar 2026 DEADLINES · LIVE REFRESH
Pulls latest regulatory deadlines via web search

Upcoming cybersecurity regulations, compliance deadlines, and enforcement milestones worldwide.

Q2 2026
Apr 15
EU DORA — Full Enforcement
Digital Operational Resilience Act now fully enforceable. ICT risk management, incident reporting, and third-party risk requirements for all EU financial entities.
EUFinServMandatory
May 1
SEC Cyber Incident Reporting — Annual Review
SEC review of material cyber incident disclosure compliance. Companies must demonstrate 4-day reporting capability.
USPublic Co
Jun 1
MiCA Phase 2 — CASPs Full Scope
Markets in Crypto-Assets regulation expands to all crypto-asset service providers. AML/KYC requirements for all exchanges operating in the EU.
EUCryptoAML
Q3 2026
Jul 1
NIS2 Enforcement Deadline — Extended
EU member states must have NIS2 transposed into national law. Mandatory incident reporting within 24 hours for essential and important entities.
EUCritical InfraMandatory
Aug 15
CIRCIA — CISA Incident Reporting Rules
Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure Act rules expected to finalize. 72-hour incident and 24-hour ransomware payment reporting.
USCritical InfraRansomware
Sep 30
APAC — Singapore MAS Technology Risk Update
Updated MAS Technology Risk Management Guidelines with expanded third-party and cloud security requirements.
APACFinServ
Q4 2026
Oct 1
NYDFS Cybersecurity Amendment — Final Phase
Final phase of NYDFS 23 NYCRR 500 amendments. Enhanced governance, access controls, and monitoring requirements for regulated financial institutions.
USFinServNY State
Dec 31
Global Travel Rule — FATF Compliance Review
FATF mutual evaluation round. Countries assessed on Travel Rule implementation for virtual asset service providers.
GlobalCryptoFATF
